items.front: What two distinct elements does FAA Order JO 7110.65 prescribe for persons providing ATC services?
items.back: Air traffic control procedures and phraseology. Procedures govern what controllers do; phraseology governs what controllers say. Both are required for standardised, safe operations.

items.front: What two conditions must both be present before an LOA can specify separation minima lower than those in FAA Order JO 7110.65?
items.back: 1) Appropriate military authority must have authorised it, AND 2) the reduced separation must be between military aircraft. Both conditions must be met simultaneously. Civilian facilities may never lower the order's minima through an LOA.

items.front: When contacting a non-ATO FAA regional office, which FAA region should a facility use — the region of its physical location or its service area office?
items.back: The region where the facility is physically located — not the region where its service area office is located. Physical geography governs over organisational alignment for non-ATO regional contacts.

items.front: Where is the text of FAA Order JO 7110.65 available online, and how does this differ from where published interpretations are available?
items.back: The order text is available on two public FAA websites: Air Traffic Plans and Publications, and Orders and Notices. Published interpretations are available on the MyFAA employee portal (an internal system). The order is public; interpretations are internal.

items.front: How does the initial submission path for a procedural change recommendation differ between FAA facility personnel and an external industry source?
items.back: FAA personnel submit recommended changes to facility management first. Recommendations from other sources (industry, military, users) go through appropriate FAA, military, or industry/user channels — not through facility management. The destination (AJV-P) is the same; the initial routing differs by source type.

items.front: At exactly 135 degrees angular difference with intersecting protected airspaces, are two aircraft on crossing courses or opposite/reciprocal courses?
items.back: Crossing courses. Crossing includes 45 through 135 degrees inclusive. Opposite/reciprocal starts above 135 degrees (greater than 135). The 135-degree boundary falls within crossing, not opposite/reciprocal.

items.front: What is the function of a note in FAA Order JO 7110.65, and does a note create independent mandatory requirements?
items.back: A note sets forth statements of fact or prefatory/explanatory material relating to directive material. Notes explain or provide context — they do not create independent mandatory requirements. The directive text governs; notes support understanding of it.

items.front: What three conditions permit a controller to deviate from JO 7110.65 procedures and minima?
items.back: 1. Conformance with ICAO Documents, National Rules of the Air, or special agreements where the U.S. provides ATC service outside its territory. 2. Other procedures/minima prescribed in an LOA, FAA directive, or military document. 3. Deviation necessary to assist an aircraft with a declared emergency.

items.front: What factors may preclude a controller from providing additional ATC services?
items.back: Traffic volume, frequency congestion, quality of surveillance, controller workload, higher priority duties, and the physical inability to scan and detect situations. Additional services are not optional but are required only when the work situation permits.

items.front: When several competing controller duties arise simultaneously, what must be performed first?
items.back: Separating aircraft and issuing safety alerts. This is the first priority. All other provisions must be prioritized using good judgment based on the situation.

items.front: How does a civil air ambulance flight trigger MEDEVAC priority handling from ATC?
items.back: The pilot must verbally state 'MEDEVAC' followed by the FAA authorized call sign or the full civil registration in radio transmissions. A 'MEDEVAC' entry in the flight plan remarks alone is informational only and does not trigger priority.

items.front: What is the operational difference between 'immediately' and 'expedite'?
items.back: 'Immediately' — compliance required to avoid an existing imminent situation. 'Expedite' — compliance required to avoid the development of an imminent situation. Restating a clearance without 'expedite' cancels the expedite instruction.

items.front: When may a controller discontinue issuing further safety alerts?
items.back: Once the pilot informs the controller that action is being taken to resolve the unsafe situation. The pilot then has sole prerogative to determine what course of action, if any, will be taken.

items.front: When no second aircraft is available for a NAVAID malfunction report, what additional information must be given to technical operations?
items.back: The time of the initial aircraft report and the estimated time a second aircraft report could be obtained. This is in addition to requesting advice on whether the aid should be shut down.

items.front: Can ATC facilities invoke or deny MARSA for military operations?
items.back: No. ATC facilities do not invoke or deny MARSA. MARSA is a military command prerogative. ATC's sole responsibility is to provide separation between MARSA aircraft and nonparticipating IFR aircraft.

items.front: For which IFR aircraft must wake turbulence separation minima continue to touchdown?
items.back: All IFR aircraft not making a visual approach or maintaining visual separation. Aircraft on a visual approach or maintaining visual separation are excluded from the touchdown continuation requirement.

items.front: What three elements must a wake turbulence cautionary advisory include?
items.back: 1. Position of the aircraft creating the wake hazard. 2. Altitude, if known. 3. Direction of flight.

items.front: How long must UAS advisories continue after the last pilot-reported or tower-observed UAS activity report?
items.back: At least 15 minutes following the last report. Advisories are issued to potentially impacted aircraft during this period.

items.front: When does ATC separation responsibility resume after a pilot reports a TCAS RA?
items.back: When: (1) the aircraft returns to its assigned altitude; OR (2) the crew reports the TCAS maneuver is complete and approved separation is observed reestablished; OR (3) the aircraft executes an alternate clearance and approved separation is observed reestablished.

items.front: When does ATC separation responsibility resume after an aircraft responds to a TAWS alert?
items.back: When: (1) the aircraft returns to its assigned altitude; OR (2) the crew reports the TAWS maneuver is completed and approved separation is observed reestablished; OR (3) the aircraft executes an alternate clearance and approved separation is observed reestablished.

items.front: What system format is used for exchanging flight plan data between U.S. ARTCCs and Canadian ACCs?
items.back: NADIN II input format as described in NAS Management Directives. Transport Canada ACC Flight Strip Format is an alternative when data fits within the strip form field size.

items.front: What is the maximum number of alphanumeric characters for an aircraft identification on a flight progress strip?
items.back: Seven alphanumeric characters. All identification formats must use combinations not exceeding seven characters.

items.front: What types of conditions must controllers emphasize in PIREP dissemination?
items.back: Strong frontal activity, squall lines, thunderstorms, light to severe icing, wind shear, turbulence (including CAT) of moderate or greater intensity, braking action, volcanic eruptions/ash clouds, and sulfur gas detection.

items.front: What three specific circumstances require ground control to notify or advise local control?
items.back: 1) Aircraft taxied to a runway other than one previously designated as active. 2) Aircraft taxied to an intersection for takeoff. 3) Before releasing an aircraft when runways or aircraft are not visible — advise the aircraft's location first.

items.front: What are the three authorized uses of a certified tower radar display by local controllers?
items.back: 1) Determine aircraft identification, exact location, or spatial relationship to other aircraft. 2) Provide radar traffic advisories. 3) Provide suggested headings or directions to VFR aircraft as an advisory aid to navigation (not radar vectors).

items.front: What does each ATC light signal mean for an aircraft in flight?
items.back: Steady green = cleared to land. Flashing green = return for landing. Steady red = give way, continue circling. Flashing red = airport unsafe, do not land. Alternating red/green = exercise extreme caution. Used when radio communications cannot be employed.

items.front: What additional detail must accompany a volcanic ash report in timely airport condition information?
items.back: State whether the ash is wet or dry, if known. Dry ash may require minimum use of reverse thrust; wet ash may degrade braking. No other condition in the timely-information list requires this specific physical-state descriptor.

items.front: What four actions must the controller take while Braking Action Advisories are in effect?
items.back: 1) Issue latest braking report to each arrival and departure. 2) If no report for the runway, issue 'NO BRAKING ACTION REPORTS RECEIVED.' 3) Advise Airport Operator of good-to-medium, medium, medium-to-poor, poor, or nil reports. 4) Solicit PIREPs of braking action.

items.front: What triggers the emergency airport lighting rule, and is a formal declaration required?
items.back: Controller awareness that an emergency has occurred OR will occur. No formal declaration (MAYDAY) is required. Reasonable anticipation of an emergency from observed conditions or pilot reports is sufficient.

items.front: When must runway edge lights be operated during daytime hours?
items.back: Between sunrise and sunset when surface visibility is less than 2 miles. Apply the same operational pattern as nighttime: turn on before aircraft taxies onto runway (departures) or begins final approach/enters surface area (arrivals), and keep on until aircraft leaves the surface area.

items.front: What weather minimums trigger POFZ clearance versus OCS clearance, and why do they differ?
items.back: POFZ: aircraft within 2 miles AND ceiling below 300 ft OR visibility below 3/4 SM. OCS: aircraft within 2 miles AND ceiling below 800 ft OR visibility below 2 SM. POFZ criteria are stricter because it protects a smaller, closer-in volume for immediate missed approach.

items.front: What is anticipating separation for takeoff clearances?
items.back: Issuing a takeoff clearance before prescribed separation exists, based on reasonable assurance that separation WILL exist when the aircraft starts its takeoff roll. It does not relax separation standards — only the timing of the clearance.

items.front: What phraseology must accompany a landing clearance when the controller cannot visually spot a reporting aircraft?
items.back: "NOT IN SIGHT, RUNWAY (number) CLEARED TO LAND." This tells the pilot the controller lacks visual contact. The clearance may still be issued if separation is assured through other means.

items.front: May a controller withhold a landing clearance indefinitely to address an apparent regulatory violation?
items.back: No. The apparent violation may stem from an emergency. The controller must assist the pilot. The landing clearance must not be used as an enforcement tool.

items.front: What is the prerequisite for applying Section 12 sea-lane provisions?
items.back: Sea lanes must be both established AND controlled. Uncontrolled waterways do not trigger the section, even if floatplanes use them. The provisions apply only where formal sea-lane infrastructure exists.

items.front: When does an aircraft that has been in radar contact resume normal position reporting over compulsory reporting points?
items.back: The aircraft resumes normal position reporting when ATC informs it 'radar contact lost' or 'radar service terminated.' Until one of these statements is issued, the aircraft discontinues reporting over compulsory reporting points.

items.front: When is Code 7700 assigned to an emergency aircraft, and what is the prescribed phraseology?
items.back: Code 7700 is assigned when the pilot declares an emergency AND the aircraft is not radar identified. Phraseology: 'SQUAWK MAYDAY ON 7700.' ERAM displays 'EMRG' and STARS/MEARTS display 'EM' instead of '7700.' After contact is established, non-single-pilot helicopters and non-single-pilot turbojets may be requested to change to a computer-assigned code.

items.front: What emergency transponder code is associated with loss of two-way radio communications, and what alternate labels do ERAM and STARS/MEARTS display in place of the numeric code?
items.back: Code 7600. ERAM displays 'RDOF' and STARS/MEARTS display 'RF' instead of '7600' in the data block. The controller must apply the communications failure procedures in paragraph 10-4-4 when observing this code.

items.front: What emergency transponder code indicates hijack or unlawful interference, and what alternate labels do ERAM and STARS/MEARTS display?
items.back: Code 7500. ERAM displays 'HIJK' and STARS/MEARTS display 'HJ' instead of '7500' in the data block. The controller must apply the hijacked aircraft procedures in paragraph 10-2-6 when observing this code.

items.front: What five non-discrete beacon codes must be monitored in addition to assigned codes, and under what condition may this monitoring be temporarily discontinued?
items.back: Code 1200, Code 1202, Code 1203, Code 1255, and Code 1277. Monitoring may be temporarily discontinued during periods when excessive VFR target presentations derogate the separation of IFR traffic. These codes need not be monitored if the area of responsibility includes only Class A airspace.

items.front: In terminal operations, what minimum angular difference is required between aircraft courses to apply passing or diverging separation, and how does this differ by sensor type?
items.back: Single site ASR or FUSION mode: at least 15 degrees. Single site ARSR or FUSION mode when ISR is displayed: at least 45 degrees. All other approved separation may be discontinued when these criteria are met and tracks are monitored to ensure targets will not touch.

items.front: How much supplemental separation is added for a standard formation flight, and why is it required?
items.back: Add 1 mile to the appropriate radar separation minima. Required because the distance allowed between formation aircraft and the lead aircraft means the formation periphery may extend beyond where standard separation measures from the lead.

items.front: To which three categories of aircraft must speed adjustment NOT be assigned?
items.back: 1. Aircraft at or above FL 390 without pilot consent. 2. Aircraft executing a published high altitude instrument approach procedure. 3. Aircraft in a holding pattern. Additionally, the pilot may refuse any speed adjustment considered excessive or contrary to the aircraft's operating specifications.

items.front: What is the prescribed phraseology for assigning an initial heading to a departing aircraft that will be vectored immediately after takeoff?
items.back: FLY RUNWAY HEADING. Or: TURN LEFT/RIGHT, HEADING (degrees). The initial heading must be consistent with either the SID being flown or the DVA, if applicable. Concurrent use of both a SID and DVA is not permitted.

items.front: What constraint applies to headings assigned for final approach course interception?
items.back: Headings must permit final approach course interception on a track that does not exceed the interception angles specified in TBL 5-9-1. The maximum interception angle depends on the distance from the approach gate and whether the approach is vectoring to an intermediate fix.

items.front: What are the two conditions under which an approach clearance may be issued to an arriving aircraft being vectored to the final approach course?
items.back: 1. The aircraft is established on a segment of a published route or instrument approach procedure. 2. The aircraft is assigned an altitude to maintain until established on a segment of a published route or instrument approach procedure. These conditions apply except when conducting a radar approach.

items.front: During simultaneous dependent approaches, what diagonal separation is required when runway centerlines are between 3,600 and 8,300 feet apart?
items.back: A minimum of 1.5 miles radar separation diagonally between successive aircraft on adjacent final approach courses.

items.front: What separation must be provided between aircraft during turn-on to parallel final approaches for simultaneous dependent approaches?
items.back: A minimum of 1,000 feet vertical or a minimum of 3 miles radar separation between aircraft during turn on.

items.front: What are the runway centerline separation and final approach course offset requirements for Simultaneous Offset Instrument Approaches (SOIA)?
items.back: Parallel runways with centerlines separated by at least 750 feet and less than 3,000 feet, with one final approach course offset by 2.5 to 3.0 degrees. SOIA may only be conducted at FAA designated airports with authorization from the Director, Strategic Operations, AJT-1, in coordination with AFS.

items.front: What runway centerline separation defines widely-spaced parallel runways for simultaneous independent approaches, and how does field elevation affect the threshold?
items.back: More than 9,000 feet between runway centerlines with a field elevation at or below 5,000 feet MSL; or 9,200 feet between runway centerlines with a field elevation above 5,000 feet MSL. Approaches may only be conducted where instrument approach charts specifically authorize simultaneous approaches.

items.front: During simultaneous approaches with reduced separation, if one aircraft executes a go-around, what must the controller do and when?
items.back: Expeditiously issue control instructions to increase separation between the applicable aircraft PRIOR TO LOSING the approved reduced separation. These instructions must establish approved separation (altitude and/or lateral via divergence). Wake turbulence cautionary advisories must also be issued per 2-1-20.

items.front: Does a pilot's acceptance of a radar approach waive the prescribed weather minima for the airport or aircraft operator?
items.back: No. Acceptance of a radar approach by a pilot does not waive the prescribed weather minima. The pilot is responsible for determining if the approach and landing are authorized under the existing weather minima.

items.front: What approach information item must continue to be provided on every radar approach even during repeated approaches with no changes?
items.back: The altimeter setting. All other items, except for subparagraph 3 (altimeter setting), may be omitted after the first approach if repeated approaches are made and no change has occurred. Transmissions should occur approximately every minute during this phase.

items.front: At what interval and relative to what reference are recommended altitudes provided on a surveillance final approach?
items.back: For each mile on final, at or above the published MDA, down to the minimum descent altitude or circling minimum descent altitude. Recommended altitudes are provided only if the pilot requests them.

items.front: How often should transmissions occur with an aircraft on a surveillance final approach, and what distance information must be provided?
items.back: Transmissions should occur approximately every 15 seconds. Inform the aircraft of its distance from the runway, airport/heliport, or missed approach point, as appropriate, each mile on final. Controllers should not key the radio transmitter continuously to preclude a lengthy communications block.

items.front: What is the prescribed phraseology for instructing an aircraft on a PAR/ASR approach to begin final descent?
items.back: BEGIN DESCENT. The instruction is issued when the aircraft reaches the point where final descent is to start.

items.front: What is the controller's required response when an E-MSAW alert activates, and what caution must be observed when issuing a clearance in reaction to the alert?
items.back: Immediately analyze the situation and take appropriate action to resolve the alert. When issuing a clearance in reaction to an E-MSAW alert, exercise caution to ensure that adjacent MIA areas are not a factor that would create a new terrain hazard.

items.front: When Mode C altitude is unavailable or unreliable, what are the three triggers for entering reported altitudes into the computer?
items.back: 1. When the aircraft reaches the assigned altitude. 2. When an aircraft at an assigned altitude is issued a clearance to climb or descend. 3. A minimum of each 10,000 feet during climb to or descent from FL 180 and above.

items.front: What additional altitude buffer must sector altitude limits display beyond the sector's own stratum for Merging Target Procedures, and what minimum data block content is required?
items.back: At minimum: 1,200 feet above highest and below lowest altitude/FL where 1,000-foot vertical separation applies; 2,200 feet above highest and below lowest FL where 2,000-foot vertical separation applies. The data block must contain Mode C altitude and call sign or beacon code at minimum.

items.front: When must the inhibit function (versus the suppress function) be used for CA/MCI alerts?
items.back: The inhibit function must only be used to inhibit the display of CA for aircraft routinely engaged in operations where approved separation criteria do not apply, such as ADC practice intercept operations and air shows.

items.front: From what event does the 5-minute clock start for obtaining a missing position report that affects separation?
items.back: From when the aircraft was estimated over the fix — not from when the controller notices the missing report.

items.front: In terminal adjacent-airport operations, what are the four wake turbulence separation values and their corresponding lead-trail category pairs?
items.back: 3 min — any B-I behind A (a1). 2 min — any B-I behind B or D (a2). 2 min — E-I behind C (a3). 2 min — I behind E (a4).

items.front: For nonintersecting runways in terminal operations, what centerline distance is required for simultaneous takeoffs when the runways diverge by less than 15 degrees?
items.back: At least 3,500 feet between runway centerlines at and beyond the takeoff points.

items.front: What are the two authorized separation methods for same-course departures when the following aircraft will climb through the leading aircraft's altitude?
items.back: 3 minutes until the following aircraft passes through the assigned altitude of the leading aircraft; OR 5 miles between DME-equipped aircraft, RNAV-equipped using ATD, or mixed DME/ATD with the DME aircraft at/below 10,000 ft or outside 10 miles from the DME NAVAID.

items.front: What two speed advantages trigger reduced longitudinal separation minima, and what are the corresponding minima for DME/ATD-equipped aircraft?
items.back: Leading aircraft at least 44 knots faster → 5 miles. At least 22 knots faster → 10 miles. Neither speed advantage → 20 miles fallback.

items.front: What is the time window during which opposite-course aircraft must be assigned different altitudes?
items.back: From 10 minutes before until 10 minutes after they are estimated to pass.

items.front: What two conditions must be met before a controller may authorize the following aircraft to maintain longitudinal separation by pilot concurrence?
items.back: The pilots must be on the same course and in direct radio communication with each other, and they must concur.

items.front: What is the minimum divergence angle required between radials of the same NAVAID before the diverging-radials lateral separation procedure may be applied?
items.back: At least 15 degrees.

items.front: What are the DME arc minima between different arcs about the same NAVAID?
items.back: At 35 miles or less from the NAVAID: 10 miles. More than 35 miles from the NAVAID: 20 miles.

items.front: For RNAV aircraft on diverging or crossing courses, how far beyond the point where protected airspaces cease to overlap must an aircraft be for lateral separation to exist?
items.back: Route 8 miles or less in width: 4 miles. Route greater than 8 miles: 9 miles, or 15 miles if extended usable distance limitation beyond 130 NM applies.

items.front: What are the four exceptions that require the stricter altitude reassignment rule?
items.back: 1. Severe turbulence is reported. 2. Military aerial refueling. 3. Aircraft issued pilot's-discretion climb/descent. 4. Aircraft on a cruise clearance.

items.front: What triggers the issuance of a clearance for the succeeding aircraft in a timed-approach sequence?
items.back: When the preceding aircraft passes the final approach fix inbound (nonprecision) or the outer marker/fix used in lieu of outer marker inbound (precision).

items.front: Under what condition may the next aircraft continue a timed approach after the preceding aircraft misses?
items.back: Only if it has been assigned an alternative missed approach procedure. Otherwise it must be held or kept under radar control until traffic conditions permit issuing approach clearances.

items.front: What does a pilot's use of the phrase 'have numbers' indicate to approach control?
items.back: The pilot has received the required landing data (wind, runway, altimeter). It does NOT indicate receipt of the ATIS broadcast. The controller may omit wind, runway, and altimeter information but must still provide traffic information on a workload-permitting basis.

items.front: Under what two conditions is visual separation NOT authorized regardless of other factors?
items.back: 1. When the lead aircraft is a super. 2. When wake turbulence separation is required (for tower-applied visual separation). Additionally, tower-applied visual separation is not authorized between successive departures when departure routes and/or aircraft performance preclude maintaining separation.

items.front: What phraseology does a controller use to inform an aircraft of anticipated delay when an SVFR clearance cannot be granted because of IFR traffic?
items.back: EXPECT (number) MINUTES DELAY, (additional instructions as necessary). Do not issue an EFC or expected departure time.

items.front: In TRSA operations, what separation is required between two VFR helicopters?
items.back: Helicopters and powered-lift aircraft need not be separated from other helicopters or powered-lift aircraft. Traffic information must be exchanged as necessary. This differs from Class B and Class C helicopter exceptions which specify separation relief only from IFR helicopters (Class C) or from VFR/IFR helicopters (Class B).

items.front: What four services does Class C service include?
items.back: 1. Sequencing of all aircraft to the primary airport. 2. Standard IFR services to IFR aircraft. 3. Separation, traffic advisories, and safety alerts between IFR and VFR aircraft. 4. Mandatory traffic advisories and safety alerts between VFR aircraft.

items.front: When must a Class C controller instruct a VFR aircraft to resume appropriate VFR altitudes?
items.back: When an assigned altitude contrary to 14 CFR 91.159 is no longer needed for separation, when leaving the outer area, or when terminating Class C service. Phraseology: RESUME APPROPRIATE VFR ALTITUDES.

items.front: In Class B airspace, when must a controller instruct a VFR aircraft to resume appropriate VFR altitudes after assigning an altitude contrary to 14 CFR 91.159?
items.back: When the altitude assignment is no longer required or when leaving Class B airspace. Phraseology: RESUME APPROPRIATE VFR ALTITUDES.

items.front: What must a controller do when a situation in offshore airspace is not covered by Chapter 8?
items.back: Apply the provisions elsewhere in JO 7110.65 that address the situation. Chapter 8 applies first, but the rest of the Order fills gaps.

items.front: What is the altitude limit for VFR flight plan operations in the Miami, Houston, and San Juan Oceanic CTAs?
items.back: Below FL 180. Operations above FL 180 in these CTAs are not permitted on a VFR flight plan.

items.front: Under what condition may radar separation be used as one of the required separation types in oceanic/offshore procedures?
items.back: Radar separation may be used where radar coverage is adequate, as specified in Chapter 5, Radar.

items.front: What two circumstances permit departure from the flight levels and 29.92 inches Hg standard for altitude assignment in oceanic control areas?
items.back: When directed otherwise, or when charted otherwise. Both directives and charted procedures may prescribe a different altimeter setting basis.

items.front: Does the 10-minute action deadline apply to ALL missed position reports in oceanic airspace?
items.back: No. The deadline applies only when the position report affects separation. If a missing report does not affect separation, the 10-minute action requirement does not apply.

items.front: For which type of separation must control estimates be used?
items.back: Control estimates must be used when applying time-based separation minima. They provide the estimated position of aircraft with reference to time.

items.front: What three independent requirements must be satisfied before a non-RVSM aircraft may operate in oceanic RVSM airspace?
items.back:
1. Meet the criteria of an excepted aircraft (initial delivery, maintenance ferry for failed RVSM, mercy/humanitarian, or spare-engine-under-wing in specific FIRs).
2. Previously approved by the operations supervisor/CIC.
3. Accommodated on a workload or traffic-permitting basis.

items.front: What is the minimum estimate revision that ARTCCs must forward to the receiving ATS facility?
items.back: Revisions of 3 minutes or more. Revisions of less than 3 minutes do not require forwarding.

items.front: Under what condition does the nonradar 5-minute communications transfer rule NOT apply as written?
items.back: When the control and/or communication units concerned have agreed otherwise. The 5-minute rule applies 'unless otherwise agreed.'

items.front: What two conditions must both be met before vertical separation may be discontinued for aircraft on reciprocal tracks?
items.back:
1. Both aircraft have reported passing a significant point (or passing ground-based NAVAIDs/DME fixes indicating they have passed each other).
2. The aircraft are separated by at least the applicable same-direction longitudinal minimum.

items.front: What is the operational purpose of the Mach number technique?
items.back: It allows for the application of reduced longitudinal separation minima by ensuring aircraft maintain assigned Mach numbers, enabling more accurate prediction of relative positions.

items.front: For intersecting flight paths with constant but different width protected airspace, how is the applicable lateral separation minimum determined?
items.back: It is the sum of the protected airspace of both flight paths, measured perpendicular to the flight path of the other aircraft. Each aircraft's protected airspace width is added together.

items.front: Compare the reduced route protected airspace below FL 240 versus at and above FL 240. What are the widths and distances?
items.back: Below FL 240: 5 miles each side to 57.14 miles from NAVAID. At/above FL 240: 10 miles each side to 114.29 miles. Both then expand on a 5-degree angle. Higher altitude = double the width and distance.

items.front: Compare the minimum divergence angles for track separation: VOR/VORTAC/TACAN vs NDB vs Dead Reckoning.
items.back:
VOR/VORTAC/TACAN: 15 degrees minimum divergence.
NDB: 30 degrees minimum divergence, and one aircraft at least 15 miles from the NAVAID.
Dead Reckoning: 45 degrees minimum divergence, and one aircraft at least 15 miles from the intersection point.

items.front: What operational risk does the altitude/flight level transition rule below FL 180 address?
items.back: The risk that differences between standard altimeter setting (QNE, 29.92) and local altimeter setting (QNH) could compromise vertical separation when aircraft cross the offshore/oceanic boundary.

items.front: What are the two timing conditions for an outbound aircraft to climb through opposite-direction oceanic traffic during offshore-to-oceanic transition?
items.back:
1. Vertical separation established before the outbound crosses the offshore/oceanic boundary.
2. Vertical separation established 15 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.

items.front: What are the first three conditions for the same-direction 5-minute climb-through procedure in offshore-to-oceanic transition?
items.back:
1. Preceding aircraft is level at assigned altitude with speed equal to or greater than the following.
2. Minimum of 5 minutes maintained between preceding and following.
3. Following aircraft separated by NOT MORE THAN 4,000 feet from preceding when climb clearance issued.

items.front: What are the final two conditions for the same-direction 5-minute climb-through procedure in offshore-to-oceanic transition?
items.back:
4. Following aircraft commences climb within 10 minutes after passing the exact reporting point or radar-observed position of the preceding.
5. Following aircraft is in direct communication with ATC until vertical separation is established.

items.front: When using vertical separation from a temporary stationary airspace reservation, how is the protected area defined?
items.back: The stationary reservation PLUS a buffer around the perimeter equivalent to one-half the lateral separation minimum.

items.front: What is the governing rule for ATC services in the North Atlantic ICAO Region?
items.back: Services are provided with the procedures and minima contained in Section 7 of Chapter 8, except when noted otherwise. Section 7 procedures take precedence over general Chapter 8 for this region.

items.front: What is the source for vertical separation procedures in the North Atlantic ICAO Region?
items.back: Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification. This is the same source referenced by all four ICAO regional vertical separation paragraphs.

items.front: In the North Atlantic ICAO Region, what are the basic longitudinal minima for each aircraft category not using Mach number technique?
items.back:
Supersonic (different types/cruise): 15 minutes.
Turbojet subsonic: 15 minutes (or Mach technique).
Nonturbojet in WATRS: 20 minutes.
Nonturbojet outside WATRS: 30 minutes.

items.front: What are the three narrowest lateral separation tiers in the North Atlantic ICAO Region?
items.back:
1. 23 NM — approved aircraft (min RNP 4, RCP 240, RSP 180) with CPDLC/voice and ADS-C.
2. 50 NM — RNP 4 or RNP 10 approved in New York/San Juan/Atlantic Miami CTAs/FIRs.
3. 60 NM or 1° latitude — supersonic above FL 275, or MNPS/NAT HLA aircraft.

items.front: What are the two widest lateral separation tiers in the North Atlantic ICAO Region?
items.back:
4. 90 NM or 1.5° latitude — non-RNP aircraft on WATRS/San Juan/Atlantic Miami routes, or U.S./Canada-Bermuda-Caribbean routes west of 55°W.
5. 120 NM or 2° latitude — all other aircraft not covered by the narrower tiers.

items.front: In the Caribbean ICAO Region, what longitudinal minimum applies to turbojet aircraft at or above FL 200 versus below FL 200?
items.back: At or above FL 200 in listed CTAs/FIRs: 15 minutes (or Mach technique). Below FL 200 in San Juan (outside WATRS), Miami, and Houston CTAs/FIRs: 20 minutes. The minimum doubles below FL 200 for turbojets in these areas.

items.front: How does the Caribbean lateral separation scheme differ east versus west of 55 degrees West?
items.back: West of 55°W: non-RNP aircraft 90 NM; others not covered by narrower tiers 100 NM. East of 55°W: 120 NM for aircraft not covered by narrower tiers. The eastern Caribbean has a wider base lateral minimum.

items.front: What two conditions must both be satisfied before an IFR flight may be cleared to climb or descend in VFR conditions in the Houston, Miami, or San Juan CTA?
items.back:
1. The climb or descent must be requested by the pilot.
2. It must be between sunrise and sunset.
Both conditions must be met; neither alone is sufficient.

items.front: In the Pacific ICAO Region, what vertical separation window is required for reciprocal track aircraft without lateral separation?
items.back: Vertical separation must be provided at least 10 minutes before and after the time the aircraft are estimated to pass or are estimated to have passed. This creates a minimum 20-minute vertical separation window centered on the passing point.

items.front: What makes the North American ICAO Region application paragraph unique compared to the other three regional application paragraphs?
items.back: It is the only regional application paragraph that does NOT include the phrase 'except when noted otherwise.' It states services are provided with the procedures and minima in Section 10 without a stated exception mechanism.

items.front: What are the two sources that govern vertical separation in the North American ICAO Region?
items.back:
1. Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification.
2. Facility directives depicting the transition between flight levels and metric altitudes.
This is the only regional section that requires facility directives as a second source.

items.front: In the North American ICAO Region, what are the time-based longitudinal minima for each aircraft category?
items.back:
Turbojet: 15 minutes (or Mach technique).
Other (nonturbojet): 20 minutes.
Simpler than other regions — only two basic time-based categories.

items.front: When transiting SUA under an LOA/LOP, what two controller obligations exist?
items.back: 1. Comply with the using agency's instructions or clearances. 2. Provide applicable separation minima between aircraft when two or more are transiting the area.

items.front: What is the controller's fallback when unable to comply with using agency instructions during a coordinated SUA transit?
items.back: Clear the aircraft in accordance with nonparticipant separation minima (9-3-2): at least 500 feet vertical (1,000 feet above FL 290), 3 miles radar, or 3 miles lateral from the SUA boundary.

items.front: What is the relationship between the controller and the aircraft commander during a dropsonde release?
items.back: The controller advises the mission aircraft of traffic near the drop area. The aircraft commander decides whether to delay the dropsonde release — based solely upon the traffic information ATC provided.

items.front: What are the two distinct controller duties when a pilot's route or landing involves a hazardous radiation area?
items.back: 1. Flight path: Advise the pilot and reroute if requested. 2. Landing airfield: Inform the pilot the airfield is within the area and request the pilot to advise his/her intentions.

items.front: When a pilot's intended landing airfield is within a hazardous radiation area, what does the controller request?
items.back: The controller requests the pilot to advise his or her intentions. The controller does not automatically reroute or close the airport — the pilot decides how to proceed.

items.front: What three actions must the first ATC facility take when establishing contact with a FLYNET aircraft?
items.back: 1. Provide expeditious handling. 2. Report to the OS/CIC for DEN notification to the NTSO ATSC. 3. Add to the flight plan remarks that DEN notification has been accomplished.

items.front: Which aircraft can receive radar vectoring to a jettison drop area without a written agreement?
items.back: USAF, Air National Guard, and Air Force Reserve aircraft. Civil operators and civil aircraft companies require a written agreement in effect for the location.

items.front: What is required before civil operators can receive vectoring service for jettisoning external stores?
items.back: A written agreement must be in effect for the location. The regional counsel must approve the agreement, which relieves the FAA of possible liability.

items.front: What level of accommodation must controllers provide to SAMP aircraft requesting altitude or route changes?
items.back: Approve inflight clearance requests for altitude and route changes to the maximum extent possible. Other IFR aircraft may be recleared so that SAMP requests are approved.

items.front: What is the operational difference between 'dangerous cargo' and 'inert devices' in a military flight plan?
items.back: Dangerous cargo contains hazardous materials requiring special handling. Inert devices contain NO dangerous materials but closely resemble nuclear or explosive devices in appearance — they also receive special handling to protect the mission profile.

items.front: What is the key operational difference between Class D and Class A/B/C parachute jump authorization?
items.back: Class A/B/C (9-7-2): Separate nonparticipating aircraft FROM the airspace authorized for jumping. Class D (9-7-3): Authorize jumping WITH RESPECT TO known or observed traffic — a judgment-based standard that does not require positive separation.

items.front: What are the four separation minima for nonparticipating aircraft from active SUA, ATCAA, or stationary ALTRV?
items.back: 1. At least 500 feet above/below the airspace limit (above FL 290: 1,000 feet). 2. 3 miles radar. 3. 3 miles laterally. 4. In accordance with a letter of agreement or letter of procedure.

items.front: What must ATC confirm before advising that MARSA is terminated during aerial refueling?
items.back: ATC must receive the tanker's report that both aircraft are vertically positioned within the refueling airspace. Only then does ATC advise MARSA terminated. MARSA does NOT automatically end when the receiver reports fueling complete.

items.front: What are the controller's responsibilities regarding unusual aircraft activity near special interest sites?
items.back: Immediately relay any reports or information regarding unusual aircraft activities near sites such as nuclear power plants, dams, or refineries to supervisory/CIC personnel. Controllers have no responsibility to monitor or observe aircraft near these sites unless directed.

items.front: How do Distress and Urgency conditions differ?
items.back: Distress: serious and/or imminent danger requiring immediate assistance. Urgency: concerning safety of an aircraft, vehicle, or person but not requiring immediate assistance.

items.front: What external facilities may a controller enlist for an aircraft in distress, and when?
items.back: Available radar facilities operated by FAA, military, and FCC, and their emergency services. Enlist them when the pilot requests or when the controller deems necessary.

items.front: What three categories of aircraft trigger the controller's coordination obligation?
items.back: Aircraft believed to be overdue, lost, or in emergency status. Coordinate efforts to the extent possible for all three categories.

items.front: What hazards can nearby aircraft create for emergency rescue operations?
items.back: Congestion, distraction, and wake turbulence from nearby airplanes and helicopters. These can prevent or delay proper execution of rescue or relief operations.

items.front: When may a controller request supplementary emergency information from a low-altitude military fighter-type aircraft?
items.back: Request the aircraft's position if not visually sighted, on radar, or given by the pilot. All other supplementary items should normally not be requested from low-altitude military fighter-type aircraft.

items.front: Why is climb particularly dangerous for an aircraft inside a volcanic ash cloud?
items.back: Engine damage from ash ingestion increases as engine power increases. High-bypass turbofan engines (e.g., B747) are especially vulnerable. Climb while in the ash cloud is avoided where terrain permits.

items.front: What three steps must a controller take when a VFR aircraft in weather difficulty needs a frequency change?
items.back: 1. Advise the pilot of the reason. 2. Request contact with the appropriate facility. 3. Inform that facility of the situation. If the aircraft cannot communicate with the new facility, relay information and clearances.

items.front: What three paths allow radar assistance to a VFR pilot who encounters IFR weather?
items.back: 1. Pilot is IFR qualified/capable — file IFR and issue clearance. 2. Pilot not qualified but emergency exists — provide assistance. 3. Pilot refuses to declare emergency but controller determines emergency conditions exist — may still assist.

items.front: What is the difference between the Terminal Area EOVM and the En Route EAM?
items.back: EOVM: terminal obstruction video map. EAM: en route emergency altitude map. Both facilitate advisory service when terrain/obstacle clearance minimum altitude cannot be maintained.

items.front: What two items does the ARTCC add when forwarding an overdue aircraft report to the RCC?
items.back: 1. Facility and person calling. 2. Action taken by reporting facility and proposed action. These items are not on the terminal-to-ARTCC list.

items.front: What five time references start the 30-minute traffic restriction period for an overdue aircraft?
items.back: In priority order: 1. Approach clearance delivery time. 2. EFC time. 3. NAVAID arrival estimate. 4. Controller's current estimate. 5. Flight plan filing time (last resort).

items.front: What two requirements govern resuming normal ATC after a traffic suspension period expires?
items.back: 1. Affected operators or pilots must concur. 2. Concurrence must be maintained for 30 minutes after the suspension period expires. Withdrawal during that window suspends operations again.

items.front: When must an IFR lost-communications situation also be treated as suspicious activity?
items.back: When communications have not been reestablished after 5 minutes. Consider the aircraft's or pilot's activity suspicious and report it to the OS/CIC while continuing appropriate lost-communications actions.

items.front: How often must the debris mishap alert broadcast be reissued after a space launch or reentry vehicle mishap?
items.back: Every 5 minutes for 20 minutes following the last known event, or until the DRA is deactivated.

items.front: Where are the oceanic emergency procedures in Chapter 10 Section 6 applied?
items.back: Solely in oceanic airspace. They do not apply to domestic en route, terminal, or ground operations.

items.front: What are the three oceanic emergency phases and their primary triggers?
items.back: INCERFA: no communication within 30 min of expected time. ALERFA: subsequent comm attempts fail or aircraft fails to arrive. DETRESFA: fuel exhausted or forced landing imminent.

items.front: How do en route and terminal notification paths differ for a ground missile emergency?
items.back: En route: alerting advisories by air carrier company radio (VFR company aircraft) and FSSs adjacent to emergency. Terminal: relay all information to ARTCC and disseminate locally.

items.front: What visible hazard must all aircraft be advised to avoid near a ground missile emergency?
items.back: Any observed smoke columns. Advise all aircraft to avoid them, regardless of flight rules.

items.front: What notification is available for an extended-duration ground missile emergency, and which facility type issues it?
items.back: A Notice to Airmen (NOTAM) may be issued. This is an en route facility responsibility.

items.front: In delegated Canadian airspace, when a Chapter 12 Canadian procedure and a basic FAA procedure both address the same operation, which takes precedence?
items.back: The Chapter 12 Canadian procedure takes precedence. The rule is 'apply basic FAA procedures except for the Canadian procedures in this chapter.' The exception clause is mandatory — where a Chapter 12 procedure exists for an operation, it overrides the corresponding basic FAA procedure.

items.front: What distinguishes Canadian Class F airspace from all other Canadian airspace classes?
items.back: Class F is defined by the purpose or nature of activities within it — not by altitude limits and flight rules. It is airspace of defined dimensions where activities must be confined or where limitations are imposed on non-participating operations. Classified as advisory or restricted.

items.front: For the 1,000-feet-on-top clearance in Canadian airspace, are the two issuance conditions an AND or an OR requirement?
items.back: Both conditions must be met — they are an AND requirement: (a) the pilot requests the clearance AND (b) the pilot will not operate in Class A or Class B airspace. If either condition is not satisfied, the clearance cannot be issued.

items.front: During a Canadian lost-communications event, what special timing rule applies when a turbine operation will enter U.S. airspace within 10 minutes after passing the clearance limit?
items.back: The climb to the border-crossing altitude commences at the estimated time of crossing the Canada/U.S. boundary — earlier than the standard 10-minute hold period. This ensures the operation is at the proper altitude at the border rather than climbing through U.S. airspace.

items.front: What single prerequisite must a controller confirm exists before authorizing any parachute jump in Canadian airspace?
items.back: Prior written permission from the appropriate Canadian authority (Ministry of Transport). Without this written permission, the controller cannot authorize the jump — regardless of weather conditions, traffic volume, pilot qualifications, or verbal assurances. The permission is a prerequisite, not a factor to be weighed alongside other considerations.

items.front: Delay recording: when does Hold Data Menu or Hold View automatically record a delay?
items.back: Automatically when the aircraft is cleared out of hold. No separate controller recording action is needed at that point. Manual methods require active annotation. Per 13-1-11 NOTE.

items.front: Forecast wind data unavailable: what two specific types of data may be affected?
items.back: ALERT data AND TRAJECTORY data may both be affected. This is the key distinction from a loss of interfacility connectivity (13-1-15), where only alert data may be affected. The broader impact is because forecast winds are a direct input to trajectory modeling. Per 13-1-14.

items.front: Interfacility connectivity loss vs forecast wind loss: what data is affected in each case?
items.back: Connectivity loss: ALERT data may be affected. Forecast wind loss: ALERT AND TRAJECTORY data may be affected. Both require continued use with recognition. Per 13-1-14 and 13-1-15.

items.front: Conflict probe: what two categories of factors must controllers consider when determining a solution to a conflict probe alert?
items.back: (1) Solutions involving direct routing, altitude changes, removal of a flight direction constraint, and/or removal of a static restriction for the pertinent aircraft. (2) Impact on surrounding sector traffic and complexity levels, flight efficiencies, and user preferences. Per 13-1-2(d)(1)-(2).

items.front: ATOP conflict probe: which separation standards does conflict probe NOT support? (9 categories)
items.back: Per 13-2-2(a)(8)(a): (1) Nonintersecting paths (8-4-2a2). (2) Intersecting flight paths with variable width protected airspace (8-4-2d). (3-4) Reduction of Route Protected Airspace below/above FL 240 (8-4-3a/b). (5-6) Same NAVAID: VOR/VORTAC/TACAN and NDB (8-4-4a1/a2). (7) Dead Reckoning (8-4-4c). (8) Same Direction (8-5-4). (9) VFR Climb and Descent (8-8-5).

items.front: Weather deviation tool: what longitudinal separation paragraphs does the tool reference for RNP distance-based applications?
items.back: FAA Order JO 7110.65 paragraphs: 8-7-3 (Longitudinal Separation), 8-8-3 (Longitudinal Separation), 8-9-3 (Longitudinal Separation), and 8-10-3 (Longitudinal Separation). Also references 13-2-2 (Conflict Detection and Resolution). Per 13-2-5 references.

items.front: ATOP automated coordination: what two exceptions prevent automated coordination from constituting complete coordination?
items.back: (a) When the aircraft is in CONFLICT with another in the receiving sector. (b) When otherwise specified in FACILITY DIRECTIVES or LOA. In all other cases, automated coordination constitutes complete coordination between ATOP sectors internally and across adjacent ATOP facilities. Per 13-2-6.

items.front: What is the core operational difference between PDC and CPDLC DCL regarding amended or revised flight plans?
items.back: PDC does not permit amended or revised flight plans to be transmitted at all — they must be verbal. CPDLC DCL permits amendments to be transmitted, but those that cannot be delivered using CPDLC must be verbally issued. Per 14-1-1(d) and 14-1-2(c).

items.front: What are the character limits for DCL Selectable Fields 3, 8, and 9?
items.back: Field 3 (Climb Out): 32 characters. Field 8 (Contact): 32 characters. Field 9 (Local Information): 34 characters, and must not contradict information elsewhere in the clearance. Per 14-1-3(a)(3), (8), and (9).

items.front: What must the controller ensure regarding trajectory-altering clearances before transferring an aircraft's communications?
items.back: Ensure there are no trajectory-altering clearances (TAC) open prior to transfer of communication, unless otherwise coordinated. Per 14-2-1(e).

items.front: When does a WILCO response to a CPDLC speed assignment satisfy the pilot concurrence requirement of JO 7110.65, 5-7-2b?
items.back: When the aircraft is at or above FL 390. Below FL 390, pilot concurrence must be obtained through other means. Per 14-2-4(b)(1).

items.front: Why must clearing an aircraft out of holding be done via voice rather than CPDLC?
items.back: A route uplink does not specify a new clearance limit. Clearing an aircraft from holding requires providing a clearance limit, which CPDLC route uplinks cannot convey. Per note to 14-2-4(c)(1).

items.front: What is the prescribed phraseology for initial voice contact with an aircraft after uncertainty about CPDLC clearance delivery?
items.back: "(Call Sign) CPDLC Failure, (message)." Per 14-3-3(e) phraseology. This preface alerts the pilot that the controller is following up on a potential CPDLC delivery issue.
